article_title: Anatomical considerations in selective amygdalohippocampectomy techniques for refractory temporal lobe epilepsy: a cadaveric study with emphasis on white matter tract anatomy
label: Fig. 4
caption: Subtemporal approach. A-E. A A retractor is placed under the brain, identifying the collateral sulcus. A cortical incision is made near the uncus (yellow), and care is taken to preserve the inferior anastomotic vein (blue square) and the oculomotor nerve (green rectangle), which is medial to the tentorial incisura (light blue rectangle). B A dissector (red circle) is placed from above within the lateral ventricular sulcus to present the projection of the temporal horn on the basal surface. C En bloc removal of the hippocampal body, similar to the trans-middle temporal approach, reveals structures of the tentorial incisura. D The yellow arrow shows the trajectory using the trans-middle temporal gyrus approach. This approach follows the trajectory of fibers forming the sagittal stratum, including the IFOF (white dot), anterior commissure, and optic radiation (green dot), while staying above the ILF (red dot). E The subtemporal approach preserves the IFOF and optic radiation but may interrupt ILF fibers

article_title: Anatomical considerations in selective amygdalohippocampectomy techniques for refractory temporal lobe epilepsy: a cadaveric study with emphasis on white matter tract anatomy
label: Fig. 5
caption: Transsylvian approach. A-E. A A pterional craniotomy is performed. The sylvian fissure (red dots) is opened from the internal carotid artery bifurcation to 2 cm beyond the MCA bifurcation. B The inferior temporal trunk (yellow dots) of the M2 segment of the MCA is identified and mobilized near the limen insulae. C Corticotomy within the inferior limiting sulcus reveals the amygdala and exposes the temporal horn (yellow). D The anterior choroidal artery (light blue circle) is preserved, and its branches to the hippocampus are coagulated. Care is taken to avoid injury to the cerebral peduncle (pink). E The approach passes through the limen insulae (black rectangle), risking interruption of the UF (yellow dots), IFOF (white dots), and anterior commissure. F The yellow arrow shows the trajectory using the thanssylvian approach

article_title: Analysis of Pontine cavernous malformation resection based on 3D microanatomical study
label: Fig. 1
caption: A-C Subtemporal Transtentorial Approach (A): Schematic of the Subtemporal Transtentorial Approach craniotomy bone flap. (B): Following elevation of the temporal base, the tentorium is observed covering the underlying pons and cerebellum. The tentorial incision (dashed black line) initiates posterior to the trochlear nerve entry point and extends along the projection of the superior petrosal sinus (SPS). (C): After tentorial opening, the trigeminal nerve (CN V) and trochlear nerve (CN IV) are visualized. The ventrolateral pontine region superior to CN V is directly exposed. A lateral pontine incision is performed along the brainstem axis (incision along black dashed line) D-F Retrosigmoid Approach (D): Schematic of the retrosigmoid craniotomy. (E): Exposure range of the retrosigmoid approach, extending superiorly to the trochlear nerve (CN IV) and inferiorly to the lower cranial nerves (CN IX-XI). (F): Endoscopic view of the cerebellopontine angle anatomy. Pontine entry is performed between the trigeminal and vestibulocochlear nerves (incision along black dashed line) G-I Extreme Lateral Supracerebellar Infratentorial Approach (G): Schematic of the craniotomy for the extreme lateral supracerebellar infratentorial approach. (H): Exposure through the supracerebellar transtentorial space. The trochlear nerve (CN IV) emerges inferolateral to the inferior colliculus and courses ventrally. The superior cerebellar artery (SCA) courses adjacent to CN IV. (I): Inferior mobilization of the SCA avoids injury to its descending branches. Using CN IV as the superior boundary landmark for the interpeduncular sulcus (IPS), the incision is made along the dotted black line. Note the preservation of the lateral mesencephalic vein (LPMV).PCA Posterior Cerebral Artery, SPS Superior Petrosal Sinus, CNIII Oculomotor Nerve, CN IV Trochlear Nerve, SCA Superior Cerebellar Artery, PET.V petrosal vein, CN VIII vestibulocochlear nerve, LPMV lateral mesencephalic vein, MCP middle cerebellar peduncle, SCP superior cerebellar peduncle, IPS interpeduncular sulcus

article_title: Analysis of Pontine cavernous malformation resection based on 3D microanatomical study
label: Fig. 4
caption: Exposure ranges of surgical approaches and safety entry zones (Yellow quadrilateral: Superior trigeminal quadrangular space. Purple quadrilateral: Inferior trigeminal quadrangular space. Red arrow: Retrosigmoid approach accessing both superior and inferior trigeminal quadrangular space. Blue arrow: Subtemporal transtentorial approach accessing the superior trigeminal quadrangular space. Green arrow: Extreme lateral supracerebellar infratentorial approach accessing the interpeduncular sulcus.) BA basilar artery. BA basilar artery

article_title: Analysis of Pontine cavernous malformation resection based on 3D microanatomical study
label: Fig. 5
caption: A-C Preoperative magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) demonstrates hemorrhagic stroke within the right PCMs. D Stereotactic guidance was employed to define the surgical trajectory. E-H A right subtemporal transtentorial approach was utilized, followed by intraoperative electrophysiological mapping to identify non-eloquent cortex. I-K Brainstem entry was performed via the supra-trigeminal safe entry zone. L Postoperative imaging confirmed complete excision of the cavernous malformation. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of cavernous malformation

label: Figure 6
caption: (A) Bone flap. (B) The thinned sphenoid ridge is demonstrated following basal drilling with the aid of a dissector.

label: Figure 7
caption: After the dural incision was made, the dura was elevated and suspended. (A). Illustration, (B). Cadaver footage.

article_title: Exploring the Lamina Terminalis: A Stepwise Anatomical Comparison of Pterional and Orbitozygomatic Craniotomy Approaches
label: Figure 8
caption: (A) Exposure of the lamina terminalis. (B) After Sylvian fissure dissection, retractors provided access to the anterior and middle skull base. (C) The lamina terminalis cistern was reached by removing the anterior communicating artery using a dissector [yellow star].

article_title: Exploring the Lamina Terminalis: A Stepwise Anatomical Comparison of Pterional and Orbitozygomatic Craniotomy Approaches
label: Figure 10
caption: The skin flap was retracted anteriorly, and the superficial temporal artery (A) and facial nerve branches (B) were carefully dissected and mobilized with a dissector.
